scientificCalculators.title: Scientific Calculator: Measurement Analysis
scientificCalculators.subtitle: 📊 Statistical Analysis of Experimental Data
scientificCalculators.description: Enter your measurements, visualize statistics, and export your results. Ideal for physics and chemistry labs, and scientific experiments.

apostilaCalculator.title: Apostila-Based Calculator (Simplified)
apostilaCalculator.subtitle: Based on the Experimental Physical Chemistry workbook - UNIFAL-MG

intro.title: 📏 What is Measurement?
intro.content: Measurement is comparison. When measuring a quantity using the same instruments, generally, discordant results are obtained. These discrepancies are an inherent characteristic of any experiment because: we perform the measurements and we are human; the precision of the instruments is limited; the environment interferes with the results.

errors.title: 🔬 Classification of Experimental Errors
errors.content:
Gross Errors: resulting from lack of practice and carelessness of the experimenter in readings, calculations, and handling of materials.

Systematic Errors: parallax errors, poorly established scales, and instruments not "zeroed". These errors affect results always in the same direction.

Random Errors: unpredictable errors resulting from variations in environmental conditions (pressure, temperature, etc.). Random factors can lead to errors in both directions.

definitions.title: 📊 Basic Definitions
definitions.content:
Most probable value (Vmp): is the arithmetic mean of the series of measurements taken: Vmp = (X₁ + X₂ + ... + Xₙ)/n

Absolute deviation (δ): absolute value of the difference between the obtained result and the most probable value: δ = |X - Vmp|

Mean absolute deviation (δ̄): arithmetic mean of the absolute deviations: δ̄ = (Σδ)/n

Mean relative deviation (δᵣ): δᵣ = δ̄ / Vmp

Final representation: X = Vmp ± δ̄

operations.title: ➕ Operations Rules with Significant Figures
operations.content:
Addition and Subtraction: The result must have the same number of decimal places as the least precise term.

Multiplication and Division: The result must have the same number of significant figures as the least precise measurement.

Exponentiation and Root Extraction: The result retains the same number of significant figures as the base or radicand.
operations.formula: Rounding rule: ≥ 5 rounds up; < 5 rounds down.

table.title: 📋 Measurements Table
table.emptyHint: Add measurements using the form above

probableMean.label: Probable Mean
probableMean.tooltip: Arithmetic mean of measured values: sum of all values divided by the number of measurements.

meanAbsoluteDeviation.label: Mean Absolute Deviation
meanAbsoluteDeviation.tooltip: Average of absolute deviations from the mean. Represents the average dispersion of data.

standardDeviation.label: Standard Deviation
standardDeviation.tooltip: Measure of dispersion indicating how much values deviate from the mean. Higher values indicate more dispersed data.

relativeUncertainty.label: Relative Uncertainty
relativeUncertainty.tooltip: Uncertainty expressed as a percentage. Allows comparison of precision across different experiments.

finalResult.label: Final Result
finalResult.tooltip: Result expressed as: mean value ± uncertainty.

statistics.varianceNote: Using sample variance (n-1) to estimate population variance

messages.minValues: ⚠️ Add at least 2 measurements to view statistical results
messages.minValuesApostila: ⚠️ Add at least 1 measurement to view results

uncertainty.title: 🔬 Measurement Uncertainty (Instrumental)
uncertainty.content: Instrumental uncertainty relates to the precision of the measuring instrument. For analog instruments, consider half of the smallest scale division. For digital instruments, consider the display resolution (last significant digit).
uncertainty.example: Example: A ruler with millimeter scale has uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. A digital timer showing hundredths has uncertainty of ±0.01 s.

meanDeviation.title: 📈 Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)
meanDeviation.content: Mean absolute deviation (also called average absolute error) is the average of absolute deviations of each value from the mean. It represents the average dispersion of data and is a simple measure of experimental uncertainty.
meanDeviation.example: Example: For measurements [10.2, 10.3, 10.1], mean = 10.2, deviations: [0.0, 0.1, 0.1], MAD = 0.07

standardDeviation.title: 📊 Standard Deviation (σ)
standardDeviation.content: Standard deviation is the most commonly used measure of dispersion in statistics. It indicates how much values deviate from the mean. For small samples (n < 30), we use sample variance (n-1) for a better population estimate.

significantFigures.title: 🔢 Significant Figures
significantFigures.content: Significant figures are the reliable digits of a measurement, including the first uncertain digit. The number of significant figures indicates the precision of the measurement.
significantFigures.formula: Rule: All non-zero digits are significant; zeros between digits are significant; leading zeros are not; trailing zeros after decimal are significant.
significantFigures.example: Example: 0.00450 has 3 significant figures (4,5,0). 1500 can have 2, 3 or 4 depending on context (1.5×10³, 1.50×10³, 1.500×10³).

rounding.title: 🔄 Scientific Rounding
rounding.content: Rounding should be done on the final result, maintaining consistency with the uncertainty. The uncertainty is typically rounded to 1 or 2 significant figures.
rounding.formula: Rule: If the digit to be eliminated is <5, keep it; if >5, round up; if =5, round to the nearest even number.
